description:
Red maple is one of the most common and widespread deciduous trees of eastern North America. It is found in a variety of habitats from dry ridges to wet bottomlands. The leaves are opposite, simple, and palmately lobed with 3-5 lobes separated by shallow sinuses. In autumn, the foliage turns brilliant red, orange, or yellow.

The tree produces small red flowers in late winter or early spring, before the leaves emerge. It is fast-growing and tolerant of a wide range of soil conditions, making it one of the most widely planted native trees in North America.

White oak is a large, long-lived hardwood tree native to eastern North America. It is a dominant tree in oak-hickory forests. The leaves are deeply lobed with rounded tips, distinguishing them from red oak leaves which have pointed tips. The bark is light gray and flaky.

White oak acorns are an important mast crop for wildlife including deer, turkey, and black bear. The tree commonly lives for several hundred years and can reach 100 feet in height.

Eastern white pine is the largest pine in eastern North America, commonly reaching 80 feet tall and occasionally exceeding 150 feet. It is identified by its five long, soft, flexible blue-green needles in bundles. The cones are long and slender (4-8 inches), often curved.

White pine grows in a variety of habitats but prefers well-drained, sandy soils. It is commercially valuable for lumber and was historically over-harvested throughout its range.

Sweet birch (also called Black Birch or Cherry Birch) is a medium to large hardwood tree native to eastern North America. The bark on young trees and branches has a distinctive wintergreen scent when scratched. The bark is dark reddish-brown and does not peel like paper birch.

The leaves are alternate, simple, ovate, and doubly serrate. Sweet birch is found on cool, moist slopes and ravines in the Appalachian region.

American beech is a large, graceful tree recognized by its smooth, slate-gray bark, which remains smooth throughout the tree's life. The leaves are ovate with sharp, forward-pointing teeth. In autumn, the leaves turn golden bronze and often remain on the tree through winter.

Beech nuts (beechnuts) are an important mast food for wildlife. The tree is shade tolerant and is a dominant species in beech-maple forest communities.

Rosebay rhododendron is a large, evergreen shrub to small tree native to the Appalachian Mountains and eastern United States. It forms dense thickets in moist, shaded forest understories. The leaves are large (4-10 inches), leathery, and dark green, with leaves curling inward during cold weather.

The large pinkish-white flowers bloom in late spring to early summer (June-July). It is the largest native rhododendron in the eastern US and can persist for decades.

Tree of heaven is a fast-growing, deciduous tree native to China, introduced to North America in the 1700s. It is considered a highly invasive species that rapidly colonizes disturbed habitats, roadsides, and urban areas. It produces allelopathic chemicals that inhibit the growth of surrounding plants.

The leaves are large and pinnately compound (1-4 feet long) with 10-40 leaflets. The leaflets have distinctive glandular teeth at their base that emit an unpleasant odor when crushed. The tree produces large clusters of winged seeds (samaras).
